focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Aminul kept lifting his folder clear of the table before sweat from his wrist could creep into the forms, then setting it down again in the same careful square, as if neatness might spread by contact. Under the schoolhouse roof, late-Friday heat had softened every paper edge in the room. The wall behind him was already patched with old tape and newer notices. Outside, goats nosed the damp steps. Inside, the queue had thinned for prayers, but the air had not. It held body salt, wet jute, mildew from opened document bundles, and the sharp iron smell of people waiting to hear whether their names still belonged to them.

Halima had worked since dawn with a dryness in her mouth she could not wash away. She kept her thumb on one corner of each sheet while Aminul read the lines aloud, because the papers wanted to curl back into themselves and because her hands wanted work. When her hands were still, she heard too much.

People had begun lowering their voices when she looked up. The hush was new. The gossip itself was not. Kadam Char had always talked like a shared courtyard, one mouth picking up where another left off. But until this week the talk around her had still carried some ordinary need in it: a fever, a labor pain, a question about whether a baby ought to be nursing so little in this heat. Now the lowered voices carried appetite.

Mofiz Uddin came to the table with a widow from the eastern rise and two boys scrubbed harder than boys liked to be scrubbed. The widow kept pinching the corner of her sari over and over at the same place, worrying a tear she had already stitched once with blue thread. One of the boys had river mud still dried inside his left ear. Mofiz, in a cream panjabi that had somehow stayed clean where everybody else's cuffs had gone gray,...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ith a notebook in her lap, writing by lantern because two more families had sent names after dark. Her eyes were red from smoke. She did not look up when Halima entered. She said only, "Kashem bhai sent a boy. He wants to know if a man can witness a father he never met."

"What did you tell him?"

"That men do worse things every day."

Halima hung her wet shawl on the door peg. Water dripped off it onto the packed earth in a line as straight as writing. She listened to the rain hit the roof hard enough to shake dust from the rafters. On the shelf above her head lay Nura's certificates, Rahima's rescued tin, Jamila's carefully wrapped packet, three school cards from another lane, and a marriage deed so damp the seal had begun to smell of glue again.

Across the lane, two men who had not sat in the same courtyard in years were huddled under one sheet of plastic while a woman fed them dates from memory and slapped the wrong ones out of the air. Noorjahan's stopped watch flashed at Rahima's doorway as she argued witness price without lowering her voice. At Majed's house a lantern moved from one room to another and then held still, as if Jamila had planted it above the papers and dared the weather to take the house around them instead. Whatever else the rain spared, it had not washed the edge off the question Jamila had asked at noon.

Halima stood in the middle of her room with the smell of wet silt and mold rising around her and understood that the char had already begun to sort itself before any officer came back with a list: into the households that could gather enough paper, enough witnesses, enough swallowed insult to sound single and clean for one morning, and the households whose only honest shape was contradiction. The rain hit every roof alike. The files would not.
